abstract:
We present DeepSeek-V3, a strong Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language model with
671B total parameters with 37B activated for each token. To achieve efficient
inference and cost-effective training, DeepSeek-V3 adopts Multi-head Latent
Attention (MLA) and DeepSeekMoE architectures, which were thoroughly validated
in DeepSeek-V2. Furthermore, DeepSeek-V3 pioneers an auxiliary-loss-free
strategy for load balancing and sets a multi-token prediction training
objective for stronger performance. We pre-train DeepSeek-V3 on 14.8 trillion
diverse and high-quality tokens, followed by Supervised Fine-Tuning and
Reinforcement Learning stages to fully harness its capabilities. Comprehensive
evaluations reveal that DeepSeek-V3 outperforms other open-source models and
achieves performance comparable to leading closed-source models. Despite its
excellent performance, DeepSeek-V3 requires only 2.788M H800 GPU hours for its
full training. In addition, its training process is remarkably stable.
Throughout the entire training process, we did not experience any irrecoverable
loss spikes or perform any rollbacks. The model checkpoints are available at
this https URL
authors: et al. (133 additional authors not shown)

full_authors.full_name: et al. (133 additional authors not shown)

keyInsights: Training a 671 billion parameter Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLM to state-of-the-art performance is feasible at a reported cost of approximately $5.576 million, setting a new benchmark for cost-efficiency in large model development.

A novel auxiliary-loss-free load balancing strategy for MoE models, which dynamically adjusts expert biases based on batch-wise load, improves performance and fosters greater expert specialization compared to traditional auxiliary losses.

The multi-token prediction (MTP) training objective consistently enhances model performance without additional inference cost and enables a 1.8x increase in Tokens Per Second (TPS) through speculative decoding.

Highly optimized distributed training infrastructure, including the DualPipe algorithm and fine-grained FP8 mixed precision, ensures remarkably stable and efficient training of extremely large-scale models, even with complex cross-node MoE configurations.
originalProblem: The persistent gap in capabilities between open-source and leading closed-source large language models (LLMs).
The prohibitive training costs and resource demands associated with developing and scaling very large LLMs.
Challenges in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures, such as expert load imbalance and high communication overhead in distributed training.
The need for more efficient training objectives and improved inference speed for large-scale LLM deployment.
results: Achieved an estimated total training cost of approximately $5.576 million for a 671B parameter MoE model, demonstrating unprecedented cost-efficiency and training stability without irrecoverable loss spikes.

DeepSeek-V3-Base consistently outperforms other open-source base models, and its chat version matches or surpasses leading closed-source models like GPT-4o and Claude-3.5-Sonnet on various benchmarks, particularly excelling in code (e.g., HumanEval-Mul, Codeforces) and mathematics (e.g., AIME 2024, MATH-500).

Multi-token prediction (MTP) was validated to improve model performance and enable a 1.8x Tokens Per Second (TPS) increase during inference due to high acceptance rates for the second predicted token.
solution: Developed DeepSeek-V3, a 671 billion parameter Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model leveraging Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA) for efficient inference and a novel auxiliary-loss-free load balancing strategy.
Implemented a multi-token prediction (MTP) training objective to provide denser training signals and facilitate speculative decoding during inference.

Utilized an optimized distributed training infrastructure featuring the DualPipe algorithm, efficient cross-node communication kernels, and fine-grained FP8 mixed precision training to maximize efficiency and stability.

Applied a multi-stage post-training pipeline including Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with reasoning data distilled from DeepSeek-R1 and Reinforcement Learning (RL) with hybrid reward models and a self-rewarding mechanism.
paper_summary.summary: DeepSeek-AI introduces DeepSeek-V3, a 671 billion parameter Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model that achieves performance comparable to leading closed-source models such as GPT-4o and Claude-3.5-Sonnet, particularly excelling in coding and mathematics. This model was trained for an estimated $5.576 million, showcasing a high level of cost-efficiency through innovations in architecture, training objectives like multi-token prediction, and a highly optimized distributed training infrastructure.

title: DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report

abstract:
We introduce MiniMax-M1, the world's first open-weight, large-scale
hybrid-attention reasoning model. MiniMax-M1 is powered by a hybrid
Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture combined with a lightning attention
mechanism. The model is developed based on our previous MiniMax-Text-01 model,
which contains a total of 456 billion parameters with 45.9 billion parameters
activated per token. The M1 model natively supports a context length of 1
million tokens, 8x the context size of DeepSeek R1. Furthermore, the lightning
attention mechanism in MiniMax-M1 enables efficient scaling of test-time
compute. These properties make M1 particularly suitable for complex tasks that
require processing long inputs and thinking extensively. MiniMax-M1 is trained
using large-scale reinforcement learning (RL) on diverse problems including
sandbox-based, real-world software engineering environments. In addition to
M1's inherent efficiency advantage for RL training, we propose CISPO, a novel
RL algorithm to further enhance RL efficiency. CISPO clips importance sampling
weights rather than token updates, outperforming other competitive RL variants.
Combining hybrid-attention and CISPO enables MiniMax-M1's full RL training on
512 H800 GPUs to complete in only three weeks, with a rental cost of just
$534,700. We release two versions of MiniMax-M1 models with 40K and 80K
thinking budgets respectively, where the 40K model represents an intermediate
phase of the 80K training. Experiments on standard benchmarks show that our
models are comparable or superior to strong open-weight models such as the
original DeepSeek-R1 and Qwen3-235B, with particular strengths in complex
software engineering, tool utilization, and long-context tasks. We publicly
release MiniMax-M1 at this https URL
authors: et al. (58 additional authors not shown)

full_authors.full_name: et al. (58 additional authors not shown)

keyInsights: Efficient linear attention mechanisms, when integrated into a hybrid architecture, can achieve competitive performance at frontier model scales while providing substantial efficiency gains for long-context reasoning.

The novel CISPO reinforcement learning algorithm, which clips importance sampling weights instead of token updates, significantly improves training efficiency and stability for complex reasoning behaviors.

Scaling context lengths during training for hybrid attention models requires a gradual, multi-staged approach and careful management of precision mismatches and pathological generation patterns to ensure stability.
originalProblem: The quadratic computational complexity of traditional softmax attention limits the ability to continuously extend reasoning processes and support very long context lengths in large language models (LLMs).
Existing large reasoning models (LRMs) struggle with high computational costs during inference for extended generations, making them prohibitively expensive for complex real-world applications.
There was a lack of open-weight, large-scale reasoning models that effectively leverage and validate computationally efficient attention mechanisms for long-context tasks.
results: MiniMax-M1 achieved a significant FLOPs reduction during inference, consuming less than 50% of FLOPs compared to DeepSeek R1 at 64K generation length and approximately 25% at 100K tokens.
The model natively supports input contexts up to 1 million tokens and generation lengths of 80,000 tokens, demonstrating an 8x context size increase over DeepSeek R1.

MiniMax-M1-80k outperformed other open-weight models and even some closed-weight models (like OpenAI o3 and Claude 4 Opus) on long-context understanding benchmarks (e.g., OpenAI-MRCR at 128k and 1M tokens), while performing comparably on general coding and agentic tool use benchmarks.
solution: The model employs a hybrid Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture with 456 billion total parameters, integrating Lightning Attention (an I/O-aware linear attention variant) in most blocks, interspersed with standard softmax attention blocks.

A multi-stage training pipeline was used, including continual pre-training on a reasoning-intensive corpus, supervised fine-tuning (SFT) with long Chain-of-Thought examples, and extensive reinforcement learning (RL).

A novel RL algorithm, ClippedIS-weight Policy Optimization (CISPO), was introduced to clip importance sampling weights rather than token updates, enhancing training stability and achieving a 2x speedup over DAPO.
paper_summary.summary: MiniMax developed MiniMax-M1, a large reasoning model leveraging a hybrid Mixture-of-Experts architecture with Lightning Attention to enable efficient scaling of test-time compute for extremely long contexts. It supports up to 1 million input tokens and 80,000 generation tokens while achieving substantial FLOPs reduction and competitive performance across various reasoning benchmarks.

title: MiniMax-M1: Scaling Test-Time Compute Efficiently with Lightning  Attention

abstract: We present DeepSeek-V2, a strong Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language model characterized by economical training and efficient inference. It comprises 236B total parameters, of which 21B are activated for each token, and supports a context length of 128K tokens. DeepSeek-V2 adopts innovative architectures including Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA) and DeepSeekMoE. MLA guarantees efficient inference through significantly compressing the Key-Value (KV) cache into a latent vector, while DeepSeekMoE enables training strong models at an economical cost through sparse computation. Compared with DeepSeek 67B, DeepSeek-V2 achieves significantly stronger performance, and meanwhile saves 42.5% of training costs, reduces the KV cache by 93.3%, and boosts the maximum generation throughput to 5.76 times. We pretrain DeepSeek-V2 on a high-quality and multi-source corpus consisting of 8.1T tokens, and further perform Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) to fully unlock its potential. Evaluation results show that, even with only 21B activated parameters, DeepSeek-V2 and its chat versions still achieve top-tier performance among open-source models.
authors: et al. (57 additional authors not shown)

full_authors.full_name: et al. (57 additional authors not shown)

keyInsights: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures, when combined with efficient routing and load balancing, can deliver strong performance with fewer activated parameters and reduced training costs.

Low-rank compression techniques within attention mechanisms (Multi-head Latent Attention or MLA) effectively mitigate the Key-Value (KV) cache bottleneck, enabling longer contexts and higher inference throughput.

Strategic bilingual data curation and sophisticated alignment techniques are crucial for developing high-performing models across multiple languages, particularly for complex tasks like reasoning and open-ended generation.
originalProblem: The high computational costs and memory requirements associated with scaling large language models (LLMs) make them expensive to train and deploy.
The Key-Value (KV) cache presents a significant memory bottleneck in Transformer architectures, limiting the practical context length and inference efficiency of LLMs.
There is a need for powerful, efficient, and versatile bilingual LLMs that perform well in both English and non-English languages, such as Chinese.
results: DeepSeek-V2 reduces training costs by 42.5% compared to DeepSeek 67B and cuts KV cache memory by 93.3%, while boosting generation throughput by 5.76 times.

The model achieves top-tier performance among leading open-source models, outperforming DeepSeek 67B, competing favorably with Mixtral 8x22B and Qwen1.5 72B, and demonstrating stronger Chinese capabilities.
DeepSeek-V2 Chat (RL) achieves an 8.97 MT-Bench score on English conversations and a 7.91 AlignBench score, significantly outperforming other open-source models in Chinese.
solution: Introduces Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA), a novel attention mechanism that compresses both keys and values into a low-dimensional latent vector to reduce KV cache memory and improve inference efficiency.
Employs an advanced DeepSeekMoE architecture with fine-grained expert segmentation, device-limited routing, and auxiliary loss functions for load balancing to enable economical training.

Pre-trained on an 8.1T token, high-quality, multi-source bilingual (Chinese and English) corpus, followed by alignment using Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO).
paper_summary.summary: DeepSeek-V2, developed by DeepSeek-AI, is a Mixture-of-Experts language model that demonstrates strong performance while achieving reduced training costs and improved inference efficiency. It delivers a 42.5% reduction in training costs and a 93.3% reduction in KV cache memory, showcasing its practicality for large-scale deployment and its top-tier capabilities in both English and Chinese.

title: DeepSeek-V2: A Strong, Economical, and Efficient Mixture-of-Experts Language Model

abstract:
We introduce Kimi K2, a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) large language model with 32 billion activated parameters and 1 trillion total parameters. We propose the MuonClip optimizer, which improves upon Muon with a novel QK-clip technique to address training instability while enjoying the advanced token efficiency of Muon. Based on MuonClip, K2 was pre-trained on 15.5 trillion tokens with zero loss spike. During post-training, K2 undergoes a multi-stage post-training process, highlighted by a large-scale agentic data synthesis pipeline and a joint reinforcement learning (RL) stage, where the model improves its capabilities through interactions with real and synthetic environments.
Kimi K2 achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source non-thinking models, with strengths in agentic capabilities. Notably, K2 obtains 66.1 on Tau2-Bench, 76.5 on ACEBench (En), 65.8 on SWE-Bench Verified, and 47.3 on SWE-Bench Multilingual -- surpassing most open and closed-sourced baselines in non-thinking settings. It also exhibits strong capabilities in coding, mathematics, and reasoning tasks, with a score of 53.7 on LiveCodeBench v6, 49.5 on AIME 2025, 75.1 on GPQA-Diamond, and 27.1 on OJBench, all without extended thinking. These results position Kimi K2 as one of the most capable open-source large language models to date, particularly in software engineering and agentic tasks. We release our base and post-trained model checkpoints to facilitate future research and applications of agentic intelligence.

keyInsights: Sparsity scaling laws indicate that increasing the total number of experts in an MoE model, while keeping active experts fixed, can consistently lower training and validation loss within computational budgets.
Training stability for trillion-parameter MoE models can be effectively managed through novel optimization techniques like MuonClip, which prevent attention logit explosions during pre-training.

Scalable agentic intelligence requires both high-quality, diverse synthetic data generation for tool use and a sophisticated reinforcement learning setup incorporating verifiable rewards, self-critique, and efficient infrastructure.
originalProblem: Limitations in existing LLMs regarding autonomous interaction with dynamic environments and performing multi-step agentic tasks.
Challenges in efficiently pre-training massive Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models, particularly ensuring training stability and maximizing the learning signal from high-quality data.
Difficulty in scaling complex agentic capabilities (multi-step reasoning, planning, tool use) due to their scarcity and variability in natural training datasets.
results: Achieved 65.8% on SWE-bench Verified (Agentic-Single-Attempt) and 53.7% on LiveCodeBench v6, leading all evaluated open-source models and surpassing GPT-4.1 in non-thinking settings.
Set new benchmarks in tool-use tasks, scoring 66.1% on Tau2-bench and 76.5% on ACEBench (en), outperforming all open-source baselines.

Ranked as the top open-source model and 5th overall on the LMSYS Arena leaderboard, demonstrating state-of-the-art win-rates on Arena-Hard-Auto v2.0 for hard prompts (54.5%) and creative writing (85.0%).
solution: Developed Kimi K2, an ultra-sparse MoE transformer architecture with 1.04 trillion parameters (32 billion activated per token), utilizing 384 experts and optimized attention mechanisms.
Introduced the novel MuonClip optimizer, combining token-efficient Muon with QK-Clip to dynamically constrain attention logits, ensuring stable pre-training without loss spikes.

Designed a multi-stage post-training process, including large-scale synthetic agentic data generation with diverse tool specifications and simulated multi-turn trajectories, along with a general reinforcement learning framework featuring verifiable rewards and self-critique mechanisms.
paper_summary.summary: Kimi K2, an open-source 1.04 trillion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts large language model from Moonshot AI, advances agentic intelligence by achieving state-of-the-art performance in competitive coding and tool use, significantly closing the gap with proprietary models. It introduces innovations in pre-training stability with the MuonClip optimizer and scales agentic capabilities through large-scale synthetic data generation and a robust reinforcement learning framework.

title: Kimi K2: Open Agentic Intelligence

abstract: This paper introduces MiniCPM4, a highly efficient large language model (LLM) designed explicitly for end-side devices. We achieve this efficiency through systematic innovation in four key dimensions: model architecture, training data, training algorithms, and inference systems. Specifically, in terms of model architecture, we propose InfLLM v2, a trainable sparse attention mechanism that accelerates both prefilling and decoding phases for long-context processing. Regarding training data, we propose UltraClean, an efficient and accurate pre-training data filtering and generation strategy, and UltraChat v2, a comprehensive supervised fine-tuning dataset. These datasets enable satisfactory model performance to be achieved using just 8 trillion training tokens. Regarding training algorithms, we propose ModelTunnel v2 for efficient pre-training strategy search, and improve existing post-training methods by introducing chunk-wise rollout for load-balanced reinforcement learning and data-efficient tenary LLM, BitCPM. Regarding inference systems, we propose this http URL that integrates sparse attention, model quantization, and speculative sampling to achieve efficient prefilling and decoding. To meet diverse on-device requirements, MiniCPM4 is available in two versions, with 0.5B and 8B parameters, respectively. Furthermore, we construct a hybrid reasoning model, MiniCPM4.1, which can be used in both deep reasoning mode and non-reasoning mode. Evaluation results demonstrate that MiniCPM4 and MiniCPM4.1 outperform similar-sized open-source models across benchmarks, with the 8B variants showing significant speed improvements on long sequence understanding and generation.

keyInsights: A comprehensive, co-designed approach across the entire LLM pipeline (architecture, data, training, inference) is crucial for achieving ultra-efficient models suitable for end devices.

Strategic data filtering (UltraClean) and high-quality SFT data generation (UltraChat v2) significantly improve "capability density," allowing comparable performance with substantially fewer training tokens.
Trainable sparse attention (InfLLM v2) can effectively manage long contexts on edge devices with minimal performance degradation and provide significant inference speedups.
originalProblem: Large Language Models typically require vast computational resources, limiting deployment to expensive cloud servers.
Deploying LLMs on end devices faces significant challenges in terms of cost, latency, privacy, and hardware constraints.
Maintaining advanced capabilities like long-context processing and deep reasoning on resource-limited edge hardware is difficult.
results: MiniCPM4-8B achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models of comparable size on various benchmarks, outperforming models like Gemma3-12B and Phi4-14B.
The 8B model attains performance comparable to Qwen3-8B using only 8 trillion training tokens, representing 22% of Qwen3's training data.
Demonstrates approximately 7x decoding acceleration on a Jetson AGX Orin for 128K-length documents compared to Qwen3-8B, showcasing robust long-context capabilities on end devices.
Achieves 100% accuracy on the 128K Needle in a Haystack task, highlighting its ability to maintain high performance with extended context windows.
solution: Introduced MiniCPM4, a holistically optimized LLM featuring concurrent innovations across architecture, training data, algorithms, and inference systems.
Developed InfLLM v2, a trainable sparse attention mechanism designed for efficient long-context processing by dynamically selecting relevant key-value blocks.
Engineered high-quality training data using UltraClean filtering and UltraChat v2 for supervised fine-tuning, alongside optimized training algorithms like ModelTunnel v2 and Chunk-wise Rollout.
Implemented efficient inference systems including CPM.cu (with FR-Spec and P-GPTQ) and ArkInfer for cross-platform deployment on diverse end devices.
paper_summary.summary: The OpenBMB/Tsinghua University team developed MiniCPM4, an ultra-efficient large language model engineered for resource-constrained end devices, integrating innovations in model architecture, training data, algorithms, and inference systems. This results in state-of-the-art performance for its size, an 8B model trained with 78% fewer tokens than comparable models, and a 7x decoding acceleration on edge GPUs for long-context tasks.

title: MiniCPM4: Ultra-Efficient LLMs on End Devices

abstract:
We introduce LongCat-Flash, a 560-billion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language model designed for both computational efficiency and advanced agentic capabilities. Stemming from the need for scalable efficiency, LongCat-Flash adopts two novel designs: (a) Zero-computation Experts, which enables dynamic computational budget allocation and activates 18.6B-31.3B (27B on average) per token depending on contextual demands, optimizing resource usage. (b) Shortcut-connected MoE, which enlarges the computation-communication overlap window, demonstrating notable gains in inference efficiency and throughput compared to models of a comparable scale. We develop a comprehensive scaling framework for large models that combines hyperparameter transfer, model-growth initialization, a multi-pronged stability suite, and deterministic computation to achieve stable and reproducible training. Notably, leveraging the synergy among scalable architectural design and infrastructure efforts, we complete model training on more than 20 trillion tokens within 30 days, while achieving over 100 tokens per second (TPS) for inference at a cost of \$0.70 per million output tokens. To cultivate LongCat-Flash towards agentic intelligence, we conduct a large-scale pre-training on optimized mixtures, followed by targeted mid- and post-training on reasoning, code, and instructions, with further augmentation from synthetic data and tool use tasks. Comprehensive evaluations demonstrate that, as a non-thinking foundation model, LongCat-Flash delivers highly competitive performance among other leading models, with exceptional strengths in agentic tasks. The model checkpoint of LongCat-Flash is open-sourced to foster community research.

keyInsights: Dynamic activation of a small subset of parameters (18.6B–31.3B out of 560B) via Zero-computation Experts can maintain high performance while significantly improving computational efficiency and cost-effectiveness.

Architectural innovations like Shortcut-connected MoE (ScMoE) can enhance training and inference efficiency by expanding computation-communication overlap without negatively impacting model quality or convergence.
A systematic multi-stage training pipeline combined with advanced data synthesis frameworks for high-difficulty tasks is critical for cultivating sophisticated agentic intelligence in LLMs.
originalProblem: The high computational cost and potential scaling slowdowns of existing large language models (LLMs) pose challenges for cost-effective development and deployment.
Inefficient resource utilization in static LLM architectures, where all parameters are activated for every token, leading to wasted computation.
Developing LLMs capable of advanced agentic behaviors, requiring complex multi-step reasoning, tool use, and high-quality training data for such tasks.
results: Achieved unprecedented pre-training efficiency, completing a 560B-parameter model on over 20 trillion tokens within 30 days, with inference costs of $0.70 per million output tokens at 100+ TPS on H800 GPUs.

Demonstrated superior performance in agentic tasks, scoring 73.68% on τ²-Bench and 24.30% on the proprietary VitaBench, alongside state-of-the-art results in instruction following (IFEval 89.65%) and mathematical reasoning (AIME25 61.25%).
Exhibited competitive general capabilities, with the base model achieving comparable or superior performance to other state-of-the-art base models and the chat model scoring 86.50 on ArenaHard-V2.
solution: Developed an innovative Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture featuring Zero-computation Experts for dynamic parameter activation and Shortcut-connected MoE (ScMoE) to maximize computation-communication overlap.

Employed a multi-stage pre-training curriculum on over 20 trillion tokens, followed by a multi-stage post-training pipeline specifically designed to enhance agentic capabilities using sophisticated data synthesis frameworks.

Implemented a comprehensive model-system co-design approach, integrating custom kernels, advanced distributed training strategies, robust fault detection, and inference optimizations like Single Batch Overlap (SBO) and speculative decoding.
paper_summary.summary: Meituan LongCat Team's LongCat-Flash, a 560-billion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model, achieves top-tier performance, particularly in agentic tasks, while demonstrating exceptional computational efficiency. It was pre-trained on 20 trillion tokens in 30 days and achieves an inference cost of $0.70 per million output tokens at over 100 tokens per second (TPS) on H800 GPUs.

title: LongCat-Flash Technical Report

abstract: We present MiMo-V2-Flash, a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model with 309B total parameters and 15B active parameters, designed for fast, strong reasoning and agentic capabilities. MiMo-V2-Flash adopts a hybrid attention architecture that interleaves Sliding Window Attention (SWA) with global attention, with a 128-token sliding window under a 5:1 hybrid ratio. The model is pre-trained on 27 trillion tokens with Multi-Token Prediction (MTP), employing a native 32k context length and subsequently extended to 256k. To efficiently scale post-training compute, MiMo-V2-Flash introduces a novel Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation (MOPD) paradigm. In this framework, domain-specialized teachers (e.g., trained via large-scale reinforcement learning) provide dense and token-level reward, enabling the student model to perfectly master teacher expertise. MiMo-V2-Flash rivals top-tier open-weight models such as DeepSeek-V3.2 and Kimi-K2, despite using only 1/2 and 1/3 of their total parameters, respectively. During inference, by repurposing MTP as a draft model for speculative decoding, MiMo-V2-Flash achieves up to 3.6 acceptance length and 2.6x decoding speedup with three MTP layers. We open-source both the model weights and the three-layer MTP weights to foster open research and community collaboration.
authors: et al. (26 additional authors not shown)

full_authors.full_name: et al. (26 additional authors not shown)

keyInsights: A hybrid attention architecture, specifically with a 5:1 ratio of Sliding Window Attention to Global Attention and attention sink bias, provides a robust and efficient solution for long-context understanding up to 256K tokens.
Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) can be effectively repurposed as a native draft model for speculative decoding, yielding significant inference speedups and enhancing training arithmetic intensity.

Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation (MOPD) enables the integration of diverse, specialized capabilities from multiple teachers into a single student model, overcoming the 'see-saw' effect and achieving stable, efficient learning.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) face a quadratic computational and memory bottleneck when processing very long contexts, limiting their scalability and efficiency.
Existing LLMs often lack robust, autonomous agentic capabilities and advanced multi-step reasoning chains needed for complex tasks like software engineering.
Traditional post-training methods for LLMs frequently suffer from 'capability imbalance' (the 'see-saw' effect) and learning inefficiency when integrating diverse skills.
results: Achieves leading open-source performance on software engineering benchmarks, scoring 73.4% on SWE-Bench Verified and 71.7% on SWE-Bench Multilingual.
Demonstrates robust long-context handling, with nearly 100% success rates on retrieval tasks up to 256K tokens and minimal performance degradation on GSM-Infinite at 128K context.

Utilizing three MTP layers, the model achieves up to a 3.6 acceptance length and 2.6x decoding speedup, while MOPD successfully combines specialized expertise, matching or exceeding the best teacher performance in domains like mathematics (AIME 2025: 94.1%).
solution: Employs a sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture with 309B total parameters (15B active per token) and a hybrid attention mechanism (Sliding Window Attention interleaved with Global Attention) for efficient long-context processing.
Integrates a lightweight Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) block for both pre-training and post-training, which also functions as a draft model for speculative decoding to accelerate inference.

Introduces Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation (MOPD), a three-stage post-training paradigm that uses token-level and outcome-based rewards from multiple specialized teacher models to integrate diverse expertise stably and efficiently.
paper_summary.summary: Xiaomi's MiMo-V2-Flash is an open-source, 309B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Large Language Model designed for strong reasoning and agentic capabilities, offering robust long-context understanding up to 256K tokens. It achieves competitive performance against larger models and up to 2.6x inference speedup through a hybrid attention mechanism, Multi-Token Prediction, and a novel Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation post-training framework.

title: MiMo-V2-Flash Technical Report

abstract: We introduce LongCat-Flash-Thinking-2601, a 560-billion-parameter open-source Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) reasoning model with superior agentic reasoning capability. LongCat-Flash-Thinking-2601 achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models on a wide range of agentic benchmarks, including agentic search, agentic tool use, and tool-integrated reasoning. Beyond benchmark performance, the model demonstrates strong generalization to complex tool interactions and robust behavior under noisy real-world environments. Its advanced capability stems from a unified training framework that combines domain-parallel expert training with subsequent fusion, together with an end-to-end co-design of data construction, environments, algorithms, and infrastructure spanning from pre-training to post-training. In particular, the model's strong generalization capability in complex tool-use are driven by our in-depth exploration of environment scaling and principled task construction. To optimize long-tailed, skewed generation and multi-turn agentic interactions, and to enable stable training across over 10,000 environments spanning more than 20 domains, we systematically extend our asynchronous reinforcement learning framework, DORA, for stable and efficient large-scale multi-environment training. Furthermore, recognizing that real-world tasks are inherently noisy, we conduct a systematic analysis and decomposition of real-world noise patterns, and design targeted training procedures to explicitly incorporate such imperfections into the training process, resulting in improved robustness for real-world applications. To further enhance performance on complex reasoning tasks, we introduce a Heavy Thinking mode that enables effective test-time scaling by jointly expanding reasoning depth and width through intensive parallel thinking.

keyInsights: Advancing LLM capabilities for complex tasks requires moving beyond intrinsic reasoning to adaptive interaction with external environments, necessitating robust tool use and feedback integration.

Scalable and diverse agentic data synthesis, both from text corpora and executable environments, is crucial for overcoming data scarcity and enabling models to acquire generalizable agentic behaviors.

An optimized, asynchronous RL framework (DORA) and curriculum-based training with noise injection are vital for efficiently scaling agentic training to large MoE models, promoting cross-environment generalization and real-world robustness. The 'Heavy Thinking' mode and Zigzag Attention provide effective ways to scale reasoning and context length at inference.
originalProblem: Limitations of intrinsic reasoning in large language models (LLMs) when solving complex real-world tasks, necessitating robust interaction with external environments.
Scarcity of high-quality, diverse agentic interaction trajectories for training, and the challenges of efficiently scaling reinforcement learning (RL) across numerous, heterogeneous environments.
Ensuring model robustness and generalization to real-world noise, imperfections, and unseen complex tool interactions beyond idealized training settings.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art open-source performance in mathematical reasoning (100% on AIME-2025 with Heavy Thinking) and agentic search (73.1% on BrowseComp, 77.7% on BrowseComp-ZH).

Demonstrated leading open-source agentic tool-use capabilities (88.2% on τ²-Bench Avg@4) and strong generalization to random complex tasks, with robustness to noisy environments improved via targeted training.

The 'Heavy Thinking' mode consistently yielded significant performance gains across complex reasoning tasks (e.g., IMO-AnswerBench from 78.6% to 86.8%), while the Zigzag Attention variant achieved a 1.5x inference speedup for contexts up to 1M tokens.
solution: A 560-billion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model utilizing a unified, end-to-end training framework that integrates data construction, environments, algorithms, and infrastructure.

A hybrid agentic data synthesis pipeline, combining text-driven knowledge extraction and environment-grounded execution, augmented with planning-oriented transformations to create diverse, executable trajectories.

A scalable asynchronous agentic reinforcement learning framework (DORA) that enables stable, efficient training across up to 32,000 concurrent environments, incorporating strategies for robustness against environmental noise and a 'Heavy Thinking' mode for enhanced test-time reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: Meituan's LongCat Team introduces LongCat-Flash-Thinking-2601, a 560-billion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model that advances agentic reasoning through a unified training framework, hybrid data synthesis, and a scalable reinforcement learning system. The model achieves state-of-the-art open-source performance across mathematical, search, tool-use, and coding benchmarks, further enhanced by its 'Heavy Thinking' inference mode.

title: LongCat-Flash-Thinking-2601 Technical Report

abstract: We introduce Step 3.5 Flash, a sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model that bridges frontier-level agentic intelligence and computational efficiency. We focus on what matters most when building agents: sharp reasoning and fast, reliable execution. Step 3.5 Flash pairs a 196B-parameter foundation with 11B active parameters for efficient inference. It is optimized with interleaved 3:1 sliding-window/full attention and Multi-Token Prediction (MTP-3) to reduce the latency and cost of multi-round agentic interactions. To reach frontier-level intelligence, we design a scalable reinforcement learning framework that combines verifiable signals with preference feedback, while remaining stable under large-scale off-policy training, enabling consistent self-improvement across mathematics, code, and tool use. Step 3.5 Flash demonstrates strong performance across agent, coding, and math tasks, achieving 85.4% on IMO-AnswerBench, 86.4% on LiveCodeBench-v6 (2024.08-2025.05), 88.2% on tau2-Bench, 69.0% on BrowseComp (with context management), and 51.0% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, comparable to frontier models such as GPT-5.2 xHigh and Gemini 3.0 Pro. By redefining the efficiency frontier, Step 3.5 Flash provides a high-density foundation for deploying sophisticated agents in real-world industrial environments.
authors: et al. (116 additional authors not shown)

full_authors.full_name: et al. (116 additional authors not shown)

keyInsights: Frontier-level agentic intelligence can be attained with a relatively low active parameter count (11B) through a co-designed architecture focused on computational efficiency.
Stable training of large sparse MoE models requires specific diagnostics and mitigation techniques to prevent issues like expert collapse and activation blow-ups.
Scalable and stable Reinforcement Learning for MoE models in long-horizon reasoning benefits from innovations like Metropolis Independence Sampling-Filtered Policy Optimization (MIS-PO).
originalProblem: Existing open-source large language models often lag behind proprietary frontier systems in complex reasoning capabilities.
Computational efficiency bottlenecks hinder the deployment of LLMs in long-context agentic tasks, particularly in resource-constrained environments.
Training large-scale sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models faces stability challenges like numerical sensitivity, expert collapse, and activation blow-ups.
results: Achieved frontier-level intelligence comparable to leading closed-source models (GPT-5.2 xHigh, Gemini 3.0 Pro) using only 11 billion active parameters from a 196 billion total parameter MoE model.

Demonstrated robust agentic capabilities, including scores of 88.2% on ""2-Bench and 74.4% on SWE-Bench Verified, alongside strong tool-usage gains (
_tool = 52.0 average).

Maintained stable training over 17.2 trillion tokens with robust diagnostics and observed consistent accuracy boosts from scalable RL (e.g., +3.2% on IMO-AnswerBench) and significant gains from test-time scaling (AIME 2025 accuracy from 97.3% to 99.9%).
solution: Designed a sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture with 196 billion total parameters and 11 billion active parameters, integrating a hybrid 3:1 Sliding-Window to Full Attention mechanism and Multi-Token Prediction.
Implemented robust training stability diagnostics and mitigation strategies, including activation clipping and explicit expert scaling, to address common MoE failure modes.
Developed a unified post-training recipe using Metropolis Independence Sampling-Filtered Policy Optimization (MIS-PO) for scalable RL and self-distillation from domain-specific expert models.
paper_summary.summary: The StepFun Team developed Step 3.5 Flash, an 11-billion active parameter Mixture-of-Experts model that achieves frontier-level intelligence comparable to leading closed-source LLMs while emphasizing computational efficiency for agentic systems. It shows robust performance across reasoning and agentic benchmarks, making sophisticated AI more deployable.

title: Step 3.5 Flash: Open Frontier-Level Intelligence with 11B Active Parameters

abstract:
We introduce OLMoE, a fully open, state-of-the-art language model leveraging
sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE). OLMoE-1B-7B has 7 billion (B) parameters but
uses only 1B per input token. We pretrain it on 5 trillion tokens and further
adapt it to create OLMoE-1B-7B-Instruct. Our models outperform all available
models with similar active parameters, even surpassing larger ones like
Llama2-13B-Chat and DeepSeekMoE-16B. We present various experiments on MoE
training, analyze routing in our model showing high specialization, and
open-source all aspects of our work: model weights, training data, code, and
logs.

keyInsights: Employing more, smaller, fine-grained experts (e.g., 64 experts activating 8) consistently improves MoE performance compared to fewer, larger experts or the inclusion of shared experts.
Token Choice routing outperforms Expert Choice, and auxiliary losses (load balancing and router z-loss) are critical for stable and performant MoE pretraining but prove detrimental during adaptation.

MoE router decisions saturate remarkably early in pretraining (approximately 60% fixed after 1% of training), and experts exhibit strong domain and vocabulary specialization, particularly in later layers.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) often entail immense computational costs for both training and inference, limiting accessibility for many researchers.
While Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures offer a pathway to improved cost-performance, existing MoE models largely lack transparency regarding training data, code, and architectural specifics.
A significant gap exists for high-performing MoE models that are fully open-source, hindering scientific understanding, reproducibility, and broader community innovation.
results: OLMOE-1B-7B, with 1.3B active parameters, achieves state-of-the-art performance among models in its active parameter class, outperforming all other open 1B models and demonstrating competitive performance with larger, denser models like Llama2-13B-Chat.
The model trains approximately 2x faster than dense LMs with equivalent active parameters to reach the same performance and achieves comparable results using ~3x fewer tokens during pretraining.

The instruction- and preference-tuned OLMOE-1B-7B-INSTRUCT achieves the highest average score among benchmarked adapted models, showing an 84% win rate on AlpacaEval and substantial gains on reasoning tasks like GSM8k.
solution: The research team developed OLMoE, a decoder-only Transformer that replaces traditional Feedforward Networks with a sparse MoE module, configured with 64 small experts per layer and activating 8 experts per token using dropless token choice routing.

The model was pretrained on a substantial 5.1 trillion token custom dataset (OLMOE-MIX) and subsequently adapted through instruction tuning and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to enhance performance and alignment.

A cornerstone of the approach is the full open-sourcing of all research artifacts, including model weights, complete training data, detailed training code, and comprehensive training logs, under permissive licenses.
paper_summary.summary: The Allen Institute for AI, in collaboration with Contextual AI and universities, introduced OLMoE, the first fully open, state-of-the-art sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language model. This model, with 6.9 billion total parameters activating 1.3 billion per token, achieves competitive performance with significantly reduced inference costs while providing complete transparency in its training data, code, and logs.

title: OLMoE: Open Mixture-of-Experts Language Models

abstract: We present LongCat-Flash-Thinking, an efficient 560-billion-parameter open-source Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) reasoning model. Its advanced capabilities are cultivated through a meticulously crafted training process, beginning with long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) data cold-start and culminating in large-scale Reinforcement Learning (RL). We first employ a well-designed cold-start training strategy, which significantly enhances the reasoning potential and equips the model with specialized skills in both formal and agentic reasoning. Then, a core innovation is our domain-parallel training scheme, which decouples optimization across distinct domains (e.g., STEM, Code, Agentic) and subsequently fuses the resulting expert models into a single, nearly Pareto-optimal model. This entire process is powered by our Dynamic ORchestration for Asynchronous rollout (DORA) system, a large-scale RL framework that delivers a greater than threefold training speedup over synchronous methods on tens of thousands of accelerators. As a result, LongCat-Flash-Thinking achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models on a suite of complex reasoning tasks. The model exhibits exceptional efficiency in agentic reasoning, reducing average token consumption by 64.5% (from 19, 653 to 6, 965) on AIME-25, without degrading task accuracy. We release LongCat-Flash-Thinking to promote further advances in reasoning systems and agentic AI research.

keyInsights: Meticulously curated long CoT data and reasoning-oriented Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) are crucial for building a strong reasoning foundation before advanced RL.
A disaggregated, multi-version policy architecture (DORA system) is highly effective for overcoming asynchronous RL training bottlenecks, enabling industrial-scale efficiency and stability.

Training domain-specific 'expert' models in parallel using RL, followed by intelligent model fusion, effectively cultivates specialized reasoning abilities (formal, agentic) while mitigating negative transfer.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) often lack robust and efficient reasoning capabilities for complex problems (logic, math, code, agentic tasks).
Scaling LLMs to achieve high performance typically incurs substantial computational costs during training and inference.
Industrial-scale Reinforcement Learning (RL) for LLMs faces challenges such as instability, hyperparameter sensitivity, and significant system overhead.
results: Achieves state-of-the-art open-source performance across benchmarks, including 99.2% on MATH-500, 67.6% pass@1 on MiniF2F-Test (18% margin over next best), and 79.4% on LiveCodeBench.
The DORA system provides over 3x training speedup compared to synchronous methods on tens of thousands of accelerators.
Demonstrates high efficiency in agentic reasoning by reducing average token consumption by 64.5% on AIME-25 with tool use, and achieves leading safety scores (e.g., 93.7% in Harmful queries).
solution: A two-phase training pipeline, starting with Long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Cold-Start Training to enhance foundational reasoning, followed by Large-Scale Reinforcement Learning (RL).

Utilizes a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture (560B total, 27B active parameters) for efficiency and a specialized DORA (Dynamic ORchestration for Asynchronous rollout) system for over 3x RL training speedup.

Incorporates a modified GRPO algorithm for RL stability, domain-parallel RL training for specialized skills (STEM, Code, Agentic), and a refined reward system including a generative reasoning process-aware reward model.
paper_summary.summary: Meituan's LongCat-Flash-Thinking introduces a 560-billion-parameter open-source Mixture-of-Experts model, with 27 billion active parameters, which achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models on diverse complex reasoning tasks, demonstrating competitive results against proprietary systems across mathematics, coding, and agentic benchmarks.

title: Introducing LongCat-Flash-Thinking: A Technical Report

abstract: We present Nemotron 3 Nano 30B-A3B, a Mixture-of-Experts hybrid Mamba-Transformer language model. Nemotron 3 Nano was pretrained on 25 trillion text tokens, including more than 3 trillion new unique tokens over Nemotron 2, followed by supervised fine tuning and large-scale RL on diverse environments. Nemotron 3 Nano achieves better accuracy than our previous generation Nemotron 2 Nano while activating less than half of the parameters per forward pass. It achieves up to 3.3x higher inference throughput than similarly-sized open models like GPT-OSS-20B and Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking-2507, while also being more accurate on popular benchmarks. Nemotron 3 Nano demonstrates enhanced agentic, reasoning, and chat abilities and supports context lengths up to 1M tokens. We release both our pretrained Nemotron 3 Nano 30B-A3B Base and post-trained Nemotron 3 Nano 30B-A3B checkpoints on Hugging Face.
authors: et al. (213 additional authors not shown)

full_authors.full_name: et al. (213 additional authors not shown)

keyInsights: The hybrid Mamba-Transformer architecture, integrated with a sparse MoE, effectively balances the strengths of different network types to achieve high accuracy and significant inference efficiency (up to 3.3x throughput increase).

Multi-environment Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) with a curriculum training approach proved more effective than extensive SFT for developing complex agentic reasoning and tool-use capabilities, offering stable learning across diverse tasks.

A selective FP8 Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) strategy, which kept sensitive layers in BF16, enabled approximately 99% accuracy recovery while contributing to substantial throughput gains and efficient memory usage, particularly for the KV cache.
originalProblem: Transformer-based Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with quadratic complexity in long contexts, leading to high computational costs during inference.
Scaling LLMs to larger parameter counts to improve performance often results in prohibitive computational demands, making deployment challenging and expensive.
Current LLMs often lack robust agentic reasoning and integrated tool-use capabilities required for complex, multi-step, and autonomous tasks.
results: Nemotron 3 Nano achieves on-par or better accuracy compared to leading open models (e.g., GPT-OSS-20B, Qwen3-30B-A3B) across a wide range of general knowledge, reasoning, coding, and chat benchmarks.

The model demonstrates up to 3.3 times higher inference throughput than similarly sized open models on a single H200 GPU and supports context lengths up to 1 million tokens, outperforming competitors in long-context benchmarks like RULER-100.

It significantly outperforms both GPT-OSS and Qwen3 in agentic reasoning and tool-use benchmarks (e.g., Terminal Bench, SWE-Bench), exhibiting enhanced capabilities for autonomous task execution and multi-step problem-solving.
solution: A hybrid Mamba-Transformer architecture was developed, combining the linear-time complexity of Mamba-2 State Space Model layers with Grouped-Query-Attention Transformer layers for efficient long-context processing.

A sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture was implemented, where only 6 out of 128 experts (activating 3.2 billion parameters out of 31.6 billion total) are active per forward pass, significantly boosting inference efficiency.

An extensive multi-stage post-training regimen, including Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Multi-Environment Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) with curriculum training, was used to enhance agentic reasoning, tool use, and long-context understanding.
paper_summary.summary: NVIDIA introduced Nemotron 3 Nano, an open and efficient 31.6B parameter (3.2B active) hybrid Mamba-Transformer model that demonstrates up to 3.3x higher inference throughput and on-par or better accuracy compared to leading open models across various benchmarks. The model supports context lengths up to 1 million tokens and excels in agentic reasoning tasks, with its weights and training resources made publicly available.

title: Nemotron 3 Nano: Open, Efficient Mixture-of-Experts Hybrid Mamba-Transformer Model for Agentic Reasoning

abstract:
We present Ring-lite, a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)-based large language model
optimized via reinforcement learning (RL) to achieve efficient and robust
reasoning capabilities. Built upon the publicly available Ling-lite model, a
16.8 billion parameter model with 2.75 billion activated parameters, our
approach matches the performance of state-of-the-art (SOTA) small-scale
reasoning models on challenging benchmarks (e.g., AIME, LiveCodeBench,
GPQA-Diamond) while activating only one-third of the parameters required by
comparable models. To accomplish this, we introduce a joint training pipeline
integrating distillation with RL, revealing undocumented challenges in MoE RL
training. First, we identify optimization instability during RL training, and
we propose Constrained Contextual Computation Policy Optimization(C3PO), a
novel approach that enhances training stability and improves computational
throughput via algorithm-system co-design methodology. Second, we empirically
demonstrate that selecting distillation checkpoints based on entropy loss for
RL training, rather than validation metrics, yields superior
performance-efficiency trade-offs in subsequent RL training. Finally, we
develop a two-stage training paradigm to harmonize multi-domain data
integration, addressing domain conflicts that arise in training with mixed
dataset. We will release the model, dataset, and code.

keyInsights: C3PO effectively mitigates RL training instability and reward collapse, which correlate with response length fluctuations and low model entropy from excessive supervised fine-tuning (SFT).

Optimal reinforcement learning (RL) performance is achieved when starting from a moderately fine-tuned SFT checkpoint with an entropy loss between 0.3 and 0.5, balancing initial capability with sufficient exploration.

A two-stage RL training paradigm, starting with mathematics and then incorporating code and science datasets, successfully resolves domain conflicts and harmonizes multi-domain reasoning capabilities.
originalProblem: Large-scale reinforcement learning (RL) for language models often suffers from training instability, particularly in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures, leading to issues like 'reward collapse' and gradient variance.
Existing state-of-the-art RL-based reasoning models lack transparency regarding their intricate training methodologies, hindering reproducibility and broader research access.

Open-source RL reasoning models typically specialize in narrow domains (e.g., mathematics or code) and struggle with generalization across diverse reasoning tasks, encountering 'domain conflicts' when data is mixed.
results: Ring-lite achieved a 76.65% average score across comprehensive benchmarks including MATH-500, AIME, LiveCodeBench, and GPQA Diamond, matching or surpassing leading dense models under 10 billion parameters while activating only 2.75 billion parameters.

C3PO consistently improved generation length stability, gradient stability, reward stability, and throughput stability compared to conventional GRPO, preventing reward collapse and ensuring efficient RL training.

The two-stage RL training strategy (math first, then code & science) successfully improved performance on challenging multi-domain benchmarks, with doubling code/science data in the second stage further increasing average performance by 1% across math and scientific tasks.
solution: The research developed Ring-lite, an open-source multi-domain reasoning model built on the Ling-lite Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture with 16.8 billion total parameters (2.75 billion activated).
A four-stage training pipeline was employed: General SFT, Long-CoT SFT for distillation, followed by a two-stage RL training (math first, then code and science) to manage domain integration.

Constrained Contextual Computation Policy Optimization (C3PO), an algorithm-system co-design enhancement of GRPO, was introduced to stabilize RL training by imposing a fixed training token budget and incorporating entropy regularization and MoE-specific losses.
paper_summary.summary: Ring-lite, developed by Inclusion AI's Ling Team, presents an open-source multi-domain reasoning model based on a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, achieving strong performance while activating fewer parameters. It introduces C3PO, an algorithm-system co-design, to stabilize reinforcement learning (RL) training and mitigate 'reward collapse' in LLMs.

title: Ring-lite: Scalable Reasoning via C3PO-Stabilized Reinforcement Learning  for LLMs

abstract:
Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) has emerged as a promising paradigm for foundation models due to its efficient and powerful scalability. In this work, we present Sigma-MoE-Tiny, an MoE language model that achieves the highest sparsity compared to existing open-source models. Sigma-MoE-Tiny employs fine-grained expert segmentation with up to 96 experts per layer, while activating only one expert for each token, resulting in 20B total parameters with just 0.5B activated. The major challenge introduced by such extreme sparsity lies in expert load balancing. We find that the widely-used load balancing loss tends to become ineffective in the lower layers under this setting. To address this issue, we propose a progressive sparsification schedule aiming to balance expert utilization and training stability. Sigma-MoE-Tiny is pre-trained on a diverse and high-quality corpus, followed by post-training to further unlock its capabilities. The entire training process remains remarkably stable, with no occurrence of irrecoverable loss spikes. Comprehensive evaluations reveal that, despite activating only 0.5B parameters, Sigma-MoE-Tiny achieves top-tier performance among counterparts of comparable or significantly larger scale. In addition, we provide an in-depth discussion of load balancing in highly sparse MoE models, offering insights for advancing sparsity in future MoE architectures.
Project page: this https URL
Code: this https URL

keyInsights: Extreme Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) sparsity, with a 40:1 total-to-activated parameter ratio, can enable top-tier LLM performance with substantially reduced active parameters.
Traditional load balancing loss mechanisms are ineffective in the lower layers of ultra-sparse MoE models, leading to routing collapse, while auxiliary-loss-free methods can worsen imbalance.

A progressive sparsification schedule is critical for stabilizing expert load balancing in highly sparse MoE architectures, ensuring training stability and preserving performance during sparsity transitions.
originalProblem: The challenge of scaling Large Language Models (LLMs) to vast parameter counts efficiently while maintaining or enhancing their capabilities.
Limitations in existing Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models regarding sparsity, often leading to knowledge redundancy and sub-optimal expert specialization.
Difficulties in achieving stable expert load balancing, especially in lower layers, when pursuing extreme sparsity in MoE architectures.
results: Pre-trained Sigma-MoE-Tiny-Base achieved top-tier performance across general, mathematics, and coding benchmarks, outperforming or matching several larger dense and less sparse MoE models (e.g., 64.81% on MMLU).
The progressive sparsification schedule successfully improved expert load balance in lower layers and maintained performance during the transition from modest to super-high sparsity.

Post-training, Sigma-MoE-Tiny demonstrated outstanding capabilities in complex reasoning (e.g., 94.6% on Math-500, 65.4% on AIME24) and code generation, often matching or exceeding models with significantly larger activated parameter counts.
solution: Designed Sigma-MoE-Tiny, a decoder-only Transformer with a super-high MoE sparsity using 96 experts per layer and activating only one expert per token, achieving a 40:1 total-to-activated parameter ratio.
Implemented Group Query Attention (GQA) and QK-Norm for inference efficiency and training stability, and used FP32 precision for the MoE gating network.

Proposed a progressive sparsification schedule, where lower layers begin training with more activated experts and gradually transition to the target 1-expert sparsity, to ensure effective expert load balancing.
paper_summary.summary: The SIGMA v-Team at Microsoft Research introduced Sigma-MoE-Tiny, an MoE language model featuring the highest sparsity among open-source models with a 40:1 total-to-activated parameter ratio (0.5 billion active out of 20 billion total). This model demonstrates top-tier performance across diverse benchmarks, often matching or surpassing larger dense and MoE counterparts, enabled by a novel progressive sparsification schedule for load balancing.

title: Sigma-MoE-Tiny Technical Report

abstract: We introduce Motif-2-12.7B-Reasoning, a 12.7B parameter language model designed to bridge the gap between open-weight systems and proprietary frontier models in complex reasoning and long-context understanding. Addressing the common challenges of model collapse and training instability in reasoning adaptation, we propose a comprehensive, reproducible training recipe spanning system, data, and algorithmic optimizations. Our approach combines memory-efficient infrastructure for 64K-token contexts using hybrid parallelism and kernel-level optimizations with a two-stage Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) curriculum that mitigates distribution mismatch through verified, aligned synthetic data. Furthermore, we detail a robust Reinforcement Learning Fine-Tuning (RLFT) pipeline that stabilizes training via difficulty-aware data filtering and mixed-policy trajectory reuse. Empirical results demonstrate that Motif-2-12.7B-Reasoning achieves performance comparable to models with significantly larger parameter counts across mathematics, coding, and agentic benchmarks, offering the community a competitive open model and a practical blueprint for scaling reasoning capabilities under realistic compute constraints.

keyInsights: Optimal RL hyperparameters are model-dependent, necessitating direct tuning on the target SFT model rather than relying on proxy models.
The effectiveness of synthetic SFT data hinges on aligning its reasoning distribution with the student model, not just its correctness from a larger teacher model.
Difficulty-aligned data filtering is crucial in RLFT to prevent 'gradient vanishing' or 'explosion' by ensuring sufficient intra-group reward variance for effective learning.
originalProblem: A persistent performance gap exists between open-weight and proprietary LLMs, especially in complex reasoning and long-context understanding.
Practitioners lack transparent and reproducible training recipes for advanced reasoning LLMs, particularly concerning stable Reinforcement Learning Fine-Tuning (RLFT).
Model collapse and instability are frequent issues when applying RLFT to improve reasoning capabilities and handle long contexts.
results: Motif-2-12.7B-Reasoning achieved performance comparable to, and in some cases surpassed, proprietary frontier models in the 30-40B parameter range on composite benchmarks like the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index (AAII).
The model delivered significant performance gains over its base Motif-2-12.7B-Instruct model across a broad suite of reasoning-heavy benchmarks, including mathematics, coding, and long-context tasks.
Empirical results on LiveCodeBench v5 explicitly showed an 11.91 Pass@1 gain when using reasoning distribution-aligned synthetic data for SFT, while misaligned data caused a 17.86 Pass@1 degradation.
solution: Implemented memory-efficient system optimizations, including hybrid parallelism and kernel-level techniques (e.g., Liger Kernel's loss function), to efficiently handle 64K-token contexts.
Developed a two-stage Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) curriculum with a synthetic data generation and verification pipeline to ensure reasoning distribution alignment and progressive context extension.

Engineered a robust Reinforcement Learning Fine-Tuning (RLFT) pipeline incorporating Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), LLM-as-a-data-filtering for difficulty alignment, mixed-policy trajectory reuse, and multi-task learning to enhance stability and efficiency.
paper_summary.summary: Motif Technologies introduces Motif-2-12.7B-Reasoning, an open-weight 12.7B-parameter model achieving reasoning capabilities comparable to 30-40B proprietary models, accompanied by a transparent, reproducible training recipe designed to overcome common challenges in fine-tuning.

title: Motif-2-12.7B-Reasoning: A Practitioner's Guide to RL Training Recipes

abstract: We introduce Ling 2.0, a series reasoning-oriented language foundation built upon the principle that every activation boosts reasoning capability. Designed to scale from tens of billions to one trillion parameters under a unified Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) paradigm, Ling 2.0 emphasizes high sparsity, cross-scale consistency, and efficiency guided by empirical scaling laws. The series includes three non-thinking (instruct) models - Ling-mini-2.0, Ling-flash-2.0, and Ling-1T - ranging from 16B to 1T total parameters and achieving up to 7-fold active-compute efficiency compared with dense counterparts. Ling 2.0 integrates coordinated innovations across model architecture, pre-training, post-training, and infrastructure: a high-sparsity MoE with MTP for efficient reasoning, reasoning-oriented data and mid-training CoT activation, reinforcement-based fine-tuning (DFT, Evo-CoT), and full-scale FP8 training with fine-grained heterogeneous pipelines. At the trillion scale, Ling-1T establishes a new Pareto frontier of reasoning accuracy versus computational efficiency, demonstrating that sparse activation, when properly aligned with reasoning objectives, enables scalable and efficient intelligence. Collectively, Ling 2.0 provides a coherent, open, and efficient foundation for advancing future reasoning and thinking models, including the Ring series built upon the same base.

keyInsights: Scalable and efficient intelligence in LLMs can be achieved through meticulously coordinated innovations across model architecture, data, training paradigms, and infrastructure.
High-sparsity Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures, when designed for reasoning, can provide dense-model-level performance with substantially reduced active computation.
Progressive and specialized data curation, combined with advanced post-training techniques, is critical for unlocking and sustaining high-level reasoning capabilities in LLMs.
originalProblem: Developing efficient and scalable large language models (LLMs) at the trillion-parameter level, where dense architectures become prohibitively expensive.
Consistently translating increased LLM capacity into reliable and enhanced reasoning performance across all training stages.
Maintaining computational efficiency, stability, and predictability while scaling LLMs to extreme sizes, essential for general-purpose AI agents.
results: Achieved a 7x active-compute efficiency leverage, with Ling-flash-2.0 (103B total, 6.1B activated) matching or surpassing dense models below 40B parameters.

Ling-1T established a new Pareto frontier for reasoning accuracy versus computational efficiency at the trillion-parameter scale, outperforming leading models on competition-level math benchmarks such as AIME 2025.

Demonstrated near-lossless accuracy (â¤0.25% gap to BF16) with full-scale FP8 training, reducing memory use by over 15% and increasing MFU by 15%, while pipeline parallelism improved throughput by 40%.
solution: Employing a unified Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) paradigm with high sparsity (3.5% activation) and multi-token prediction to achieve 7x compute efficiency.
Integrating Ling Scaling Laws, a novel Warmup-Stable-Merge scheduler, and reasoning-oriented data (Ling Math/Code, CoT) across pre-training and mid-training phases.
Implementing advanced post-training techniques like Evolutionary Chain-of-Thought (Evo-CoT) and Sentence-Level Policy Optimization (LPO) for progressive reasoning enhancement and stability.
Utilizing full-scale FP8 training and heterogeneous fine-grained pipeline parallelism for infrastructure-level efficiency, stability, and throughput improvements.
paper_summary.summary: Inclusion AI introduces Ling 2.0, an open language foundation series including a trillion-parameter model, focusing on scaling general reasoning capabilities with high computational efficiency. This work achieved a 7x active-compute efficiency leverage, establishing a new Pareto frontier for reasoning accuracy while maintaining near-lossless performance with full-scale FP8 training.

title: Every Activation Boosted: Scaling General Reasoner to 1 Trillion Open Language Foundation

abstract:
Mixture of Experts (MoE) models have emerged as a promising paradigm for
scaling language models efficiently by activating only a subset of parameters
for each input token. In this report, we present dots.llm1, a large-scale MoE
model that activates 14B parameters out of a total of 142B parameters,
delivering performance on par with state-of-the-art models while reducing
training and inference costs. Leveraging our meticulously crafted and efficient
data processing pipeline, dots.llm1 achieves performance comparable to
Qwen2.5-72B after pretraining on 11.2T high-quality tokens and post-training to
fully unlock its capabilities. Notably, no synthetic data is used during
pretraining. To foster further research, we open-source intermediate training
checkpoints at every one trillion tokens, providing valuable insights into the
learning dynamics of large language models.

keyInsights: MoE architectures can achieve dramatic efficiency gains (4x reduction in training costs) without performance compromise when properly balanced through auxiliary-loss-free approaches and dynamic expert bias adjustment

High-quality real-world data curation through sophisticated multi-stage processing outperforms approaches relying on synthetic data augmentation, as validated through direct comparisons with TxT360 dataset
Expert specialization emerges naturally in MoE models for domain-specific content (mathematics shows clear expert preference) while maintaining balanced utilization for general domains
Low-level infrastructure optimizations in communication overlap and GEMM operations provide substantial practical improvements that compound across massive training scales
originalProblem: Training and deploying large language models requires prohibitively expensive computational resources, creating barriers for broader research participation and sustainable AI development
Existing MoE models still activate too many parameters relative to their performance, limiting cost-effectiveness in both training and inference scenarios
Most current LLM development relies heavily on synthetic data augmentation, potentially compromising model robustness and introducing biases from generation artifacts
Limited transparency in large-scale model training prevents the research community from understanding training dynamics and optimizing future developments
results: Achieves comparable performance to Qwen2.5-72B across benchmarks while using only 14B active parameters vs 72B, with particular strength in Chinese tasks (C-Eval: 92.8 vs Qwen's lower scores) and mathematics
Reduces pretraining computational requirements from 340K to 130K GPU hours per trillion tokens, enabling practical deployment on single 8-GPU nodes with 40GB or 80GB memory
Custom Grouped GEMM implementation delivers 14.00% forward and 6.68% backward computation improvements over NVIDIA Transformer Engine v2.1 on H800 GPUs

Demonstrates stable training without loss spikes or rollbacks across entire 11.2 trillion token pretraining process, with successful context extension to 32K using UtK strategy and strong performance on RULER benchmark (94.9 at 8K, 92.6 at 16K context lengths)
solution: Develop a 142B-parameter MoE architecture with 128 routed experts and 2 shared experts, activating only top-6 plus shared experts (14B total active parameters) per token using DeepSeekMoE framework

Implement a meticulous three-stage data processing pipeline without synthetic data, featuring custom 1.5B models for web-type classification, line-wise clutter removal, and quality assessment across 11.2 trillion tokens
Build internal Cybertron framework with NVIDIA collaboration for interleaved 1F1B communication overlap and custom Grouped GEMM optimization achieving significant performance gains
Commit to open-sourcing intermediate checkpoints every trillion tokens to provide unprecedented training transparency and enable community research into large-scale model dynamics
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from rednote-hilab introduce dots.llm1, a 142-billion-parameter Mixture of Experts language model that activates only 14 billion parameters per token, achieving state-of-the-art performance comparable to Qwen2.5-72B while requiring 4x fewer GPU hours for pretraining (1.46 million vs 6.12 million total hours) through a sophisticated three-stage data processing pipeline that excludes synthetic data and employs custom models for web clutter removal and category balancing across 11.2 trillion high-quality tokens, with the framework featuring innovative infrastructure optimizations including interleaved 1F1B-based communication overlap developed with NVIDIA and efficient Grouped GEMM implementations that achieve 14% forward and 6.68% backward performance improvements, while demonstrating superior Chinese language capabilities (C-Eval: 92.8, CMMLU: 90.4) and strong mathematical reasoning (CMath: 79.5, HumanEval: 64.0) alongside unprecedented research transparency by open-sourcing intermediate training checkpoints at every trillion tokens to provide the community with detailed insights into large-scale MoE training dynamics.

title: dots.llm1 Technical Report

abstract: We present Megrez2, a novel lightweight and high-performance language model architecture optimized for device native deployment. Megrez2 introduces a novel cross-layer expert sharing mechanism, which significantly reduces total parameter count by reusing expert modules across adjacent transformer layers while maintaining most of the model's capacity. It also incorporates pre-gated routing, enabling memory-efficient expert loading and faster inference. As the first instantiation of the Megrez2 architecture, we introduce the Megrez2-Preview model, which is pre-trained on a 5-trillion-token corpus and further enhanced through supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. With only 3B activated and 7.5B stored parameters, Megrez2-Preview demonstrates competitive or superior performance compared to larger models on a wide range of tasks, including language understanding, instruction following, mathematical reasoning, and code generation. These results highlight the effectiveness of the Megrez2 architecture to achieve a balance between accuracy, efficiency, and deployability, making it a strong candidate for real-world, resource-constrained applications.

keyInsights: Cross-layer expert sharing effectively reduces the total parameter count of MoE models by reusing experts across multiple layers, thus improving memory efficiency without sacrificing per-token capacity.
Pre-gated routing allows for anticipatory loading of expert parameters, enabling computation and weight loading overlap, which significantly accelerates inference on memory-constrained devices.

A comprehensive multi-stage training approach, including specialized fine-tuning with turn-level loss and reinforcement learning with verified rewards, is crucial for optimizing the performance of such efficient architectures.
originalProblem: Deploying high-performance Large Language Models (LLMs) on resource-constrained devices faces an "Impossible Triangle" of balancing accuracy, fast inference, and low cost.
Conventional Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures, while computationally efficient per token, still suffer from large total memory footprints due to storing numerous specialized experts.
Sparse expert activations in MoE models can lead to fragmented parameter utilization and inefficient hardware serving on devices with limited memory bandwidth.
results: Megrez2-Preview operates with only 3 billion activated parameters per token, while storing a total of 7.5 billion parameters, demonstrating high efficiency.
The model achieved 91.7 on C-EVAL (Chinese benchmark), substantially outperforming larger models like Qwen2.5-7B (76.2) and even proprietary GPT-4o-mini (66.3).
Megrez2-Preview scored 88.0 on MBPP (Pass@1) for code generation, outperforming all other listed open-source and proprietary models, including Qwen2.5-7B (79.2) and Qwen3-8B (77.0).
solution: Introduces cross-layer expert sharing, where groups of consecutive transformer layers utilize the same expert pool, drastically reducing the total number of stored parameters.
Incorporates pre-gated routing, allowing the gating network from the preceding layer to compute routing decisions in advance, enabling efficient, overlapped loading of expert parameters.

Employs a comprehensive multi-stage training methodology, including pre-training on a 5-trillion-token corpus, supervised fine-tuning with a novel turn-level loss, and reinforcement learning with verified rewards.
paper_summary.summary: Megrez2 introduces a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture featuring cross-layer expert sharing and pre-gated routing, enabling a 7.5 billion total parameter model to operate with only 3 billion activated parameters per token. This design achieves strong performance across various benchmarks, including outperforming larger models on C-EVAL and MBPP, while being optimized for on-device deployment.

title: Megrez2 Technical Report

abstract: We present CompassMax-V3-Thinking, a hundred-billion-scale MoE reasoning model trained with a new RL framework built on one principle: each prompt must matter. Scaling RL to this size exposes critical inefficiencies-zero-variance prompts that waste rollouts, unstable importance sampling over long horizons, advantage inversion from standard reward models, and systemic bottlenecks in rollout processing. To overcome these challenges, we introduce several unified innovations: (1) Multi-Stage Zero-Variance Elimination, which filters out non-informative prompts and stabilizes group-based policy optimization (e.g. GRPO) by removing wasted rollouts; (2) ESPO, an entropy-adaptive optimization method that balances token-level and sequence-level importance sampling to maintain stable learning dynamics; (3) a Router Replay strategy that aligns training-time MoE router decisions with inference-time behavior to mitigate train-infer discrepancies, coupled with a reward model adjustment to prevent advantage inversion; (4) a high-throughput RL system with FP8-precision rollouts, overlapped reward computation, and length-aware scheduling to eliminate performance bottlenecks.
